name: Short QT Syndrome
identifyBy: Tall, peaked, narrow T waves in precordial leads
Absent or minimal ST segment - T wave immediately follows QRS
QT interval barely changes with heart rate (flat QT/RR relationship)
Risk of VF, SCD, atrial fibrillation in young patients

measurements.stSegment: Absent or minimal - T wave immediately follows QRS

management: ICD implantation for survivors of cardiac arrest or with documented spontaneous VF
Quinidine: class IA drug - prolongs QT (only drug shown effective); blocks Ito current
Avoid QT-shortening factors: hypercalcaemia, acidosis, cocaine
Genetic testing: KCNH2 (hERG), KCNQ1, KCNJ2 mutations identified
First-degree relatives should be screened with ECG
Restrict competitive sport pending specialist evaluation
pearls: Short QT syndrome: QTc <330ms + symptoms = high-risk (VF, SCD, AF in young)
Unlike hypercalcaemia short QT: congenital SQTS has tall peaked T waves + VF risk
The short QT makes it very hard to see the T wave until you look carefully - easy to miss
AF in young patients without obvious cause: consider SQTS screening
Prevalence is rare (<1 in 2000) but important because ICD is the only proven survival benefit
differentials: Hypercalcaemia (short QT but broad T, no tall peaks, electrolyte cause)
WPW (short PR due to delta wave, not short QT)
Normal ECG at fast HR (QT shortens physiologically - use QTc formula)
Digoxin effect (reverse tick ST, short QT but different context)
